run_name: NATP Module 8: Patient Care Skills — Source-First Dry Run
purpose: The purpose of this unit is to teach the students skills needed to support and/or assist the patient/resident in the areas of personal hygiene, an area of activities of daily living, and elimination. Personal hygiene or personal care is generally performed independently. The Nurse Assistant should assist with or perform personal care only when patients/residents are unable to perform a skill for themselves. Other activities included in this module are use of prosthetic devices, bowel and bladder retraining, and weighing and measuring height of the patient/resident.

objectives.objective_title: List ways to maintain healthy skin and identify at risk patients.
objectives.exact_objective_statement: List ways to maintain healthy skin and identify at risk patients.
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture A. Written test List ways to maintain healthy skin and identify at risk patients. B. Discussion B. Provides care to A. Maintain skin health C. Handout 8.10- High Risk maintain skin health and 1. Encourage well-balanced diet and fluids Factors for Skin prevent skin breakdown 2. Provide skin care Breakdown and Interventions to Prevent a. Bathe as necessary b. Rinse off soap thoroughly c. Apply lotion as necessary d. Massage healthy skin e. Keep skin clean and dry 3. Observe high-risk patients/residents for potential problems 4. Give special attention to bony prominences a. Turn and position the patient/resident correctly b. Keep pressure off of reddened or irritated areas 5. Do not massage red, purple, or irritated areas 6. Only massage healthy tissue around the area 7. Keep bed free of small objects and linen free of wrinkles 8. Keep patient/resident clean & dry 9. Prevent shearing and friction 10. Report and record observations B. Factors that place patients/residents at high risk for skin breakdown 1. Mobility/sensory problems a. Paraplegic or quadriplegic b. CVA c. Peripheral vascular disease d. Bed rest or decreased mobility e. Decreased sensation f. COPD 1) Low oxygen level 2) Continuous high Fowlers 3) Medications g. Circulatory problems Module 8: Patient Care Skills 1) Diabetes 2) Arterial disease with decreased sensation (neuropathy) 3) Poor circulation and healing 2. Elimination a. Incontinent of stool or urine b. Diarrhea c. Diaphoresis d. Dehydrated e. Leaking tubes or drainage 3. Fluid status a. Edema (swelling) b. Dehydration 4. Nutrition/body structu

objectives.objective_title: Describe causes, signs and symptoms, and staging of pressure sores.
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Describe causes, signs and symptoms, and staging of pressure sores.
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture A. Written test Describe causes, signs and symptoms, and staging of pressure B. Discussion sores. C. Handout 8.11- Staging for A. Common conditions which can lead to pressure sores pressure sores 1. Pressure leads to decreased blood flow and nutrition of an area resulting in tissue loss 2. Excessively wet or dry skin Module 8: Patient Care Skills B. Stages; signs and symptoms 1. Stage 1 a. Skin is not broken. The epidermis and dermis are intact b. Erythema (redness) that does not resolve within 15-30 minutes 2. Stage 2 a. Skin not intact b. Partial skin loss of the inner and/or outer skin layer (epidermis is damaged and part of the dermis can be involved) c. Skin can be blistered, cracked, and open with erythema or appear as a shallow crater d. No necrotic or dead tissue is present e. Wound bed is moist, pink and painful 3. Stage 3 a. Full-thickness skin loss with both epidermis and dermis gone b. Damage extends to, but not through the tissue that covers the muscle c. Can have part of dermis left with necrotic tissue d. Possible drainage 4. Stage 4 a. Full skin loss with major destruction and tissue death b. Involves subcutaneous tissues � possible fatty tissue, muscle and/or bone c. Can see pink healthy cells, necrotic tissue and eschar d. Wound can tunnel or have undermining in skin surrounding wound e. Risk that bone may become infected (osteomyelitis) Model Curriculum for Nurse Assistants - Revised September 2013 Module 8: Patient Care Skills
objectives.weight_reason: Deterministic source-density allocation from source minimum theory hours; assessment/clinical/certificate time excluded.
content_outline: Source objective statement: Describe causes, signs and symptoms, and staging of pressure sores.

Uses techniques to 1. Mobility/sensory B. Show pictures of wounds in various stages prevent pressure sores a. Control pressure by pressure-reducing devices or special C. Reports signs and beds C. Handout 8.12- Sitting on hand exercise: Sit on symptoms of pressure b. Turn every two hours at least hands for 5 minutes sore formation to Observe changes in licensed nurse c. Position with proper support of the body and limbs color, sensation, etc. d. Promote active range of motion exercises or assist D. Share examples of how patient/resident with them nursing assistants can be involved in QA/PI e. Teach or assist patient/resident using a wheel chair to activities change position frequently f. Prevent shearing and friction during lifting, moving, transferring and repositioning procedures g. Keep bed linens clean, crumb free, and avoid wrinkles h. Watch tubing (like Foley Catheter) does not cause pressure i. Remove patients/residents from toilets and bedpans promptly j. Encourage circulation by gentle massage around red area not over red area k. Check skin every eight hours l. Keep head of bed (H.O.B.) at 30 degrees as much as possible to avoid sacral pressure 2. Comfortable � including finding out whether the C. Role play patient/resident and in patient/resident is warm enough, but not too warm assistant 3. Easy to get on and off 4. Neat - looks nice and attractive � respect the patient's/resident's own individual taste and choice of what clothing to wear 5. In good condition � has no wrinkles or holes, has snaps, fasteners, buckles and buttons, which function as they should B. Clothing is personal property 1. Label clothes with patient's/resident's name and write on personal belongings list 2. Avoid cutting or tearing 3. Do not discard 4. Store in the patient's/resident's unit 5. Fold neatly or hang on hangers in closet 6. Find out whether family or facility will clean soiled clothing and put into dirty clothes container 7. Assist patient/resident or family to choose clothing that meets physical needs and looks good 8. Do not use patient's/resident's clothing or personal items for another patient/resident

objectives.objective_title: Discuss rationale for use of street clothes and guidelines for dressing or undressing a patient/resident
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Discuss rationale for use of street clothes and guidelines for dressing or undressing a patient/resident
objectives.source_excerpt: Discuss rationale for use of street clothes and guidelines for dressing or undressing a patient/resident. A. Purpose of dressing in street clothes 1. How a patient/resident looks has an effect and influences their feelings of dignity and self-esteem as well as how others perceive them 2. It encourages patients/residents to be more independent about their activities in daily living (ADL's) Module 8: Patient Care Skills B. Factors that limit a patient's/resident's ability to dress self D. Refer to Manual Skill a partially dependent 1. Limitation of body movement Procedure 8.14a- patient/resident a. Brain impairment, as in dementia, mental retardation, injury, Dressing and Undressing or coma a Patient/resident b. Weakness c. Pain E. Refer to Manual Skill d. Fractures Procedure 8.14b- e. Contractures Dressing a f. Amputation Patient/resident with an g. Paralysis Affected (Weak) Right 2. Other factors Arm a. Special equipment, such as IV's, casts or braces b. Blindness c. Psychological factors, such as depression, fear of pain C. Guidelines for dressing/undressing patient/resident 1. Provide for privacy 2. Encourage the patient/resident to do as much as possible 3. Allow patients/residents to choose what to wear 4. While assisting the patient/resident, be gentle, while supporting all extremities 5. Follow patient's/resident's individual choices in use of makeup, jewelry, perfume, etc. D. Reportable observations 1. How much help is provided 2. How patient/resident tolerated procedure 3. Any complaint from patient/resident

objectives.objective_title: Describe the purpose and general rules of care for urinary catheters
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Describe the purpose and general rules of care for urinary catheters
objectives.source_excerpt: Describe the purpose and general rules of care for urinary catheters. A. Definition of urinary catheter 1. Plastic or rubber tube used to drain or inject fluid through a body opening 2. Most commonly used to drain the bladder 3. One end is placed in the bladder and the other end is attached by tubing to a drainage bag 4. Catheters are inserted by licensed personnel using sterile technique B. Types of indwelling catheters a. "Foley" b. Retention c. Suprapubic d. Straight catheter C. Purpose of urinary catheter Module 8: Patient Care Skills 1. Complete loss of bladder control 2. Urinary retention (inability to urinate) 3. Before, during, and after surgical procedures 4. Too weak or disabled to use commode, bedpan or bathroom 5. Prevention of urine contamination on wounds and pressure ulcers 6. Not for convenience D. Complication resulting from catheters 1. Bladder infection 2. Blockage 3. General rules of catheter care a. Tubing should not be kinked or compressed (urine should flow without obstruction). Coil tubing on the bed b. Do not pull on catheter tubing c. Keep drainage bag below level of the bladder d. Drainage bag should be attached to the bed frame, not the side rail e. Never allow bag to touch the floor f. Catheter should be secured to the inner thigh g. Clean the perineal area and around the catheter, soapy water is adequate (use the procedure for catheter care preferred by facility) h. Drainage bag should be emptied and measured each shift (or more often if ordered/required) i. Report patient/resident complaints promptly to the licensed nurse j. Follow the rules o
objectives.weight_reason: Deterministic source-density allocation from source minimum theory hours; assessment/clinical/certificate time excluded.

objectives.objective_title: Describe the normal pattern of bowel movements and reportable observations
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Describe the normal pattern of bowel movements and reportable observations
objectives.source_excerpt: Describe the normal pattern of bowel movements and reportable observations. A. Each person is different and has own pattern 1. Frequency � daily to every 2 to 3 days 2. Time of day � morning or evening 3. The Nurse Assistant should note and report stool shape, size, Module 8: Patient Care Skills frequency, color, consistency, complaints of pain with Commode/Toilet defecation, etc. B. Notable observations 1. Stool shape 2. Size 3. Frequency 4. Color 5. Consistency 6. Amount 7. Complaints of pain with defecation C. Factors influencing bowel movements 1. Privacy � fear of others, hearing and/or smelling 2. Age � as patient/resident ages, more problems 3. Diet � need balanced diet with regular meals, food stimulates bowel movement 4. Fluids � need adequate fluid 5. Activity � stimulates 6. Medications � most tend to constipate 7. Personal habits 8. Disabilities D. General rules for maintaining normal elimination patterns 1. Promptly provide the bedpan, urinal, or commode, or help the patient/resident to the bathroom when the request is made 2. Assist the patient/resident to get into normal position (as much as is possible or safe) 3. Cover the patient/resident for privacy and warmth 4. Remain nearby if the person is weak or frail 5. Place the signal light and toilet tissue nearby 6. Allow the person time to carry out the elimination process 7. If patient/resident has difficulty, ask them what kinds of things they did at home to assist them, i.e., running water, reading or looking at a magazine Module 8: Patient Care Skills 8. Provide perineal care if needed A. Lecture/Discussio

objectives.objective_title: Describe the application, removal and care for "behind the ear" hearing aids
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Describe the application, removal and care for "behind the ear" hearing aids
objectives.source_excerpt: Explain the purpose of a hearing aid and the procedures for maintenance and care of the hearing aid. A. Purposes of a hearing aid 1. Makes sound louder 2. Even the best hearing aid cannot restore full, normal hearing ability a. The patient/resident may still have trouble hearing b. Always face the patient/resident when talking to him/her and speak clearly B. Parts of a hearing aid 1. Microphone- changes sound waves into electric signals and transmits sound 2. Battery and Battery compartment 3. Amplifier uses battery energy to make the sound signals strong 4. Earmold channels the sound through the external ear canal to the ear drum (tympanic membrane) Module 8: Patient Care Skills 5. Cord- connects the amplifier to the earmold 6. On/off switch-turns hearing aid on/off and may control volume C. Care of Hearing aids 1. Caution: Never wash a hearing aid or immerse in water, as it will be ruined 2. Report to licensed nurse when the hearing aid needs cleaning or appears damaged 3. Check the battery periodically 4. Never drop the hearing aid 5. Do not attempt to repair hearing aids 6. Do not expose the hearing aid to heat 7. Do not let moisture get into the hearing aid 8. Do not use any kind of hair spray or medical spray on patients/residents while their hearing aids are in place the spray can clog the microphone opening 9. Check food trays and bed linens for misplaced hearing aids D. Storage of a hearing aid 1. Turn the hearing aid off when it is not in use 2. Store a hearing aid in a well-marked container labeled with patient's/resident's name and room number 3. Label hearing a

content_outline: Source objective statement: Describe the application, removal and care for "behind the ear" hearing aids

Source excerpt support: Explain the purpose of a hearing aid and the procedures for maintenance and care of the hearing aid. A. Purposes of a hearing aid 1. Makes sound louder 2. Even the best hearing aid cannot restore full, normal hearing ability a. The patient/resident may still have trouble hearing b. Always face the patient/resident when talking to him/her and speak clearly B. Parts of a hearing aid 1. Microphone- changes sound waves into electric signals and transmits sound 2. Battery and Battery compartment 3. Amplifier uses battery energy to make the sound signals strong 4.
